source: Agent a5270b6 - Competitor mechanism quotes (Kirkland, Vice, Snell, DTC)

quotes.quote: I used to use Kirkland balls until my game got inconsistent. After reading the ball test on MGS, I switched to Maxfli Tour and couldn't be happier with the results.

The Costco golf ball is not worth your money. Honestly, I hate to be the one to tell you this, but your membership is probably better used on a rental car discount, a couple gallons of Mayo, or even their four pack of gloves.
After we ran these through our quality ball lab, the KSig scored a whopping 45 out of a hundred on the chart. That's the third lowest scoring ball in all of MyGolfSpy history.

I use the Kirkland v3 for hitting into my practice net at home. They're very poor quality golf balls. Cover durability is awful. They breakdown after a few wedge shots. Wouldn't recommend gaming these.
I tried V2 and they were really spinny. Great around the greens but sucked distance. I stopped playing them though because of wear issues. They scuffed up after just a few holes.

The big problem with Kirkland golf balls for me is that they change from year to year. I'm not talking about the evolutionary improvements the big name manufacturers incorporate into their lines. I'm talking about a completely different construction and/or manufacturer from year to year.
I played them for a while and found the covers too scruffy. A trap or even a spinning wedge took the ball out of play. So if you're chucking 4 balls a round into the shag bag where's the value?

I've been playing this ball and it's terrible. Even on my best drives this brick is 10 to 15 yards shorter than normal. IF you hit it perfect, it does have crazy spin on the green, but terrible if you mishit it.
I always found the Kirkland 3 piece ball to be noticeably shorter hitting off the driver for me. I love the price, but I'm not a big hitter and can't give up that much yardage.

I have the yellow ones and they seem to scuff up badly after only a few hits. I feel my swing is pretty consistent and every ball i hit seems to fly differently, almost like they are not balanced well.

From what I've read on MGS, the Kirkland balls spin too much for me. However, a 15 Delta difference in compression is huge to me, given that I have a moderate swing speed. One ball will feel like a rock & next will feel much softer. I don't want that inconsistency.
The only good ball Kirkland produced was the first version, the model they had to stop making & selling due to Acushnet Company (Titleist) lawsuit for patent infringement.
Man, I can't believe how bad they are in durability. One or two wedge shots and they're scuffed like crazy. They're even so bad that I've had the mantle delaminate from the core.
I personally think they feel a little harder than a Pro-V1 and definitely durability is an issue. I don't get along with them.

I played the proV1 pretty much all my life (over 25 years playing) and recently switched to the vice pro. I bought the double pack from Costco and can attest they have no durability. My sm9s instantly ripped the cover.
Cut literally tells you what happens to their covers after a single wedge.
Yep. Funny that happened to me the first time I put it in play. Par 3, hit it in the bunker, by the time I finished the hole it was cut. Never played the ball again.

The Cut Blue was one of the worst ever for durability, so I definitely wouldn't go for them. Interesting that the Gray held up really well, no durability issues at all, while the Blue would scuff if you looked at it wrong.
I still play the Snell ball. Don't care for any of the others, particularly the VICE & CUT.
Snell (would be my go to if I was going the DTC route). Vice (O.K. but not a huge fan). OnCore (F*** them). Cut (Never again).
Yes. Love them. They are basically ProV1's for almost half the price. MyGolfSpy confirmed this in testing.
I tried your Snell MTB black recently and enjoyed it very much. I found the distance equal to ProV1 at $16 less a dozen.
Very happy with them. I'm sure the premium balls ARE better, but at 4-Times the price they certainly don't make my game 4 times better.

Dean Snell is the founder and he spent more than 28 years in golf ball research and development. He is listed on 40 U.S. patents and was part of the team that developed the original Pro V1 ball for Titleist.

The main question was were the balls any good, and could they stand up to the performance of bigger brands like Titleist, Callaway, TaylorMade, and Bridgestone? I couldn't tell the difference between its performance versus a Pro V1 or any other premium golf ball.
Why purchase a dozen golf balls for $50, when you can get the same performance for $20 less?

Golf ball manufacturers pay handsomely to make sure their balls are everywhere. Television commercials, PGA Tour endorsement deals, and retail distribution all jack up the price that the consumer pays.
Quality materials + great design + no marketing fluff = high-performance ball for less money.

You can purchase the MTB Red or Black for $31.99 per dozen versus upwards of $50 for the Pro V1. Additionally, Snell offers a six-dozen value pack which brings the cost down to $27.33 per dozen. That's almost a 45% cost saving.
Agree with this. I played Snell's for years when they were a better value but now the Maxfli Tour and Tour X might be the best value at $109 for a 48 pack.
Maxfli is a store brand so DTC adjacent and the tour X is a good ball and amazing for the price.
I used to play Snell but the pandemic made getting them reliably iffy at the time so I changed to Callaway CSX.
With companies like Srixon, Volvik, and Honma making reasonably priced urethane balls I can't see myself dipping into DTC brands.
I picked up a box of Vice Pro balls at Sam's club last week, 24 balls for $29. I've played a couple of rounds with them and liked the results.
It's not worth saving $7 or $10 per dozen when I can play a premium ball for less than $200 per year.
The data collected during this test suggests that the Snell MTB Black and Titleist Pro V1 offer similar performance.
They have proven to be a little more durable than TM and the ProV and I think with the price point they come in at, $26/dozen when buying 6, I am definitely a believer.
Get Vice drip if you enjoy looking for your ball for forever in anything longer than the tightest cut grass.
The compression consistency across the sample was rated as 'Poor' with a 15-point compression range across the sample, leading to eight percent being flagged as Bad.
It is almost as if no two balls are the same and thereby potentially affecting your performance on the course.
Over the last 25 years Dean Snell has been involved with the design of some of the most important golf balls in the history of the game. During his time at Titleist he worked on the original Pro V1.
Currently Snell holds 38 U.S. Patents in golf ball technology and design.
Having worked with Tour players like Tiger Woods, Sergio Garcia, Phil Mickelson, Justin Rose, Jason Day, Dustin Johnson, and others, Snell is uniquely qualified to create Tour-centric models.
The 2 most lost balls are Kirkland and Callaway Supersoft, attesting, I believe to the high spin rate.
I hate Kirkland golf balls because there is a noticeable difference in distance, but if I have them, use em to lose em.
The Kirkland Signature golf ball, it's one of the spinniest balls, yes that's a word, that we've ever tested. And I mean ever.
If you want to avoid losing anywhere from 6 to 10 strokes on your game, or, who knows, possibly slicing the s*** out of the ball, I'd avoid the Kirkland altogether.
I bought a bunch of used Kirklands cuz I'm horrible and lose 8 balls a round. I must've gotten the old model because my god they scuff like crazy.
I like the extra spin, but the older cover is so soft and scuffs so easily.
These are exceptional golf balls at a 40-50% savings compared to other premium brands.
Manufacturers like Snell have shown that it does not have to be that way. You can save a tremendous amount of money on one of the key golf items without sacrificing any performance.
The Legato golf ball is a pretty darn solid ball and can be had from Amazon for around $30.00. I like it much better than the offerings from Vice and Cut (can't stand the Cut balls).
